system_design.text: A server is just a computer that provides data or services to other computers.
Start simple—design a system for 100 users before scaling to millions.
Latency means how long it takes for a request to travel and come back.
A database stores and organizes your app’s data.
Caching means storing frequently used data so you don’t recompute it.
CAP theorem states you can only guarantee 2 of Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance.
Load balancers distribute traffic across multiple servers.
Design APIs to be stateless for better scalability.
Netflix uses chaos engineering to test system resilience.
Sharding splits large datasets across multiple machines.
A request is just when your app asks a server for data.
Reduce unnecessary API calls to improve performance.
Throughput means how many requests a system can handle per second.
Frontend talks to backend through APIs.
Keep responses small to reduce load time.
Stateless systems scale better because they don’t store user session data.
API rate limiting prevents abuse and overload.
Use pagination instead of sending large datasets at once.
Eventual consistency allows distributed systems to remain available during failures.
Use circuit breakers to prevent cascading failures.

oops.text: A class is like a blueprint, and objects are real instances of it.
Think of objects as things and methods as actions they can perform.
Encapsulation means hiding data and exposing only what’s needed.
Inheritance lets one class reuse properties of another.
Use simple classes first before applying complex design patterns.
Polymorphism allows one interface to behave differently based on context.
Dependency Injection reduces tight coupling between components.
Favor composition over inheritance for flexibility.
Objects store both data and behavior together.
Use simple examples (like car, user) to understand OOP concepts.
Methods are just functions inside a class.
Constructors initialize objects when they are created.
Avoid putting too many responsibilities in one class.
Overriding lets subclasses change behavior of parent methods.
Interfaces define what a class can do, not how it does it.
Use abstraction to hide complex logic.
Loose coupling improves scalability and testability.
Design patterns should solve problems, not complicate them.

dbms.text: A table in a database is like a spreadsheet with rows and columns.
Each row represents one record (like one user).
SQL is a language used to talk to databases.
A primary key uniquely identifies each row.
Avoid storing duplicate data—it can cause inconsistencies.
Indexes can speed up queries by 100x but slow down writes.
Normalization reduces redundancy but can hurt performance.
Eventual consistency allows systems to scale at the cost of immediate accuracy.
Use sharding for very large datasets.
A column represents a specific type of data like name or age.
Use clear column names to make queries easier to read.
CRUD stands for Create, Read, Update, Delete.
Databases can store millions of records efficiently.
Use LIMIT to avoid fetching too much data.
Joins combine data from multiple tables.
Transactions ensure operations either fully succeed or fail.
Use indexes on frequently searched columns.
Distributed databases trade strict consistency for availability.
Partition large tables to improve performance.

networking.text: The internet is a network of computers connected worldwide.
A URL is like an address that tells your browser where to go.
HTTP is the protocol used to transfer web pages.
An IP address uniquely identifies a device on a network.
HTTPS is just HTTP with encryption for security.
TCP ensures reliable delivery of data packets.
DNS converts domain names into IP addresses.
HTTP/3 uses QUIC over UDP for faster connections.
Your browser sends requests every time you click a link.
A protocol is just a set of rules for communication.
WiFi and mobile data both connect you to the internet.
Use caching to reduce repeated network calls.
Bandwidth is the amount of data that can be transferred per second.
Latency is the delay before data transfer begins.
Optimize images to reduce network load.
Packet switching allows efficient data transfer across networks.
Use persistent connections to reduce handshake overhead.

programming.text: A variable is a container that stores data.
Break big problems into smaller steps.
A loop lets you repeat tasks without rewriting code.
Functions help reuse code instead of repeating it.
Time complexity measures how fast code runs as input grows.
Recursion is when a function calls itself.
A bug is simply an error in your code.
Read error messages carefully—they often tell you the problem.
Syntax errors happen when code doesn’t follow language rules.
Debugging is the process of finding and fixing bugs.
Practice coding daily to improve faster.
Data structures organize data for efficient access.
Arrays store elements in contiguous memory.
Choose the right data structure for better performance.
Concurrency allows multiple tasks to run simultaneously.
Avoid shared mutable state in concurrent programs.

wow_facts.text: Google processes over 8.5 billion searches every day.
Over 90% of the world’s data was created in recent years.
Netflix streams petabytes of data daily.
The internet handles zettabytes of data annually.

company_facts.text: Netflix uses CDNs to stream content closer to users.
Uber uses geospatial indexing to match riders and drivers.
Google Spanner provides globally consistent databases.

humor_facts.text: 99% of programming is wondering why your code doesn’t work.
If it works, don’t touch it.
There’s always that one function you’re scared to touch.
It worked on your machine… that’s the problem.
